abstract:
Cyber-physical systems (CPS) are a cornerstone of modern infrastructure, yet their safety is frequently compromised by operational faults and anomalies. In this study, we evaluate the performance of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs)—including Mistral 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, and Gemma 2—in identifying anomalies across two distinct domains: battery management and powertrain systems. 

Our methodology employs a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework featuring a novel two-step process: models first infer underlying operational rules from normal behavior data and subsequently apply those rules to detect system faults. During experimentation, we observed that while standard prompt designs were effective for the battery dataset, they required refinement to handle the complexities of the powertrain dataset. By adjusting the prompts to emphasize explicit rule inference, we saw a marked improvement in detection accuracy. Empirical results show that Mistral 7B achieved F1-scores of 0.99, while Llama 3.1 8B and Gemma 2 reached a perfect F1-score of 1.0 in several complex scenarios. These findings highlight the critical role of prompt engineering and rule extraction in adapting LLMs for fault detection, ultimately enhancing the operational resilience of cyber-physical systems.
